agent_text:
## Diagnosis

**The single highest-leverage change: move the retrieval / context-fetch step off the full-priced standard model onto the cheap small model.** The scratch log already ran this experiment and measured a **~14% drop in total per-doc cost** — enough to more than reverse the entire 22% QoQ rise's momentum on the line that's actually causing it.

### Why the cost is rising, and why this is the best lever

The two-step pipeline gives you two candidate levers. Rank them by *unrealized, growing* spend, not by raw size:

**Generation — the larger slice, but the wrong target.** It's *flat* quarter over quarter, so it isn't what's driving the 22% climb. And it's already been squeezed twice: it runs on the **batch-discount API path** and its context length was **capped in the Q2 trim**. Both savings are already baked into today's numbers. Attacking generation means fighting for scraps on a line that (a) isn't growing and (b) has had its two biggest knobs already turned. Low remaining leverage.

**Retrieval — smaller (~40%) but the entire growth term and completely untouched.** It's *the one line still growing*, and it still makes **full-priced calls to the standard model** — no discount path, no cheaper tier. That combination (rising + full-price + un-optimized) is exactly where leverage lives: it's the marginal dollar you haven't spent effort on yet. The model tier is the dominant cost multiplier on that step, and dropping to the small model is the biggest single price cut available to it.

**The experiment settles it empirically.** You don't have to model this — the logged batch already showed **~14% off total per-doc cost** from this one swap. Against a 22% QoQ increase that is the difference between "cost keeps climbing" and "cost is back down and the growing line is neutralized."

### Guardrails before you ship it
- **Confirm downstream quality holds.** The experiment measured *cost*; verify the small model's retrieved context doesn't degrade the generation step's output (the summaries it feeds). The 14% is only a win if quality is unchanged — measure that on the same batch, not just spend.
- **This is orthogonal to your existing per-source context cache** (keyed by document hash). That cache already skips fetches for repeated documents; the fact that retrieval cost is *still growing* suggests your corpus is mostly-unique docs where the cache rarely hits — which is exactly why cutting the *per-call* price is the right move rather than trying to squeeze more cache hits.

RECOMMENDATION: Switch the retrieval / context-fetch step from the full-priced standard model to the cheap small model (validated at ~14% total per-doc cost reduction), after confirming retrieved-context quality holds.
recommendation: Switch the retrieval / context-fetch step from the full-priced standard model to the cheap small model (validated at ~14% total per-doc cost reduction), after confirming retrieved-context quality holds.

agent_text:
## Diagnosis

The spend outran commit volume because the **largest compute slice carries a 2.6× multiplier that commit count doesn't**. Walk the breakdown:

- Unit shards: minority, flat → not the driver.
- Fixture rebuild: ~6% of the suite, already cached in Q1 → already solved, tiny.
- **Integration suite: the single largest slice — and every run executes an average of 2.6 times before it goes green.**

So each commit doesn't buy one run of the biggest suite; it buys ~2.6. That extra ~1.6× is a pure multiplier sitting on top of the largest cost center. *That* is the term that makes the bill grow faster than commits — not the commit rate, the per-commit re-execution factor.

## The false constraint

The log concludes: "we can't patch [the vendored conformance suite], so the retry multiplier is what it is." That inference doesn't hold. Two separate things are being conflated:

- **Upstream's flakiness** — genuinely fixed. You can't stop those tests from occasionally failing.
- **Your retry *granularity*** — entirely yours. Today a flaky failure in a *subset* of the conformance tests re-runs the **entire integration suite** — the deterministic 90%+ that never flaked gets re-executed 1.6 extra times to chase a green on a small flaky slice.

You don't need to touch the vendored code to stop re-running the parts that already passed. **Rerun only the tests that actually failed** (test-level retry / quarantine the flaky conformance subset onto its own retry path). That collapses the 1.6 extra attempts from "full suite" to "just the handful of flaky tests" — a near-total removal of the multiplier on the largest slice, in *real compute*, not billing rate.

## Why this beats the preemptible-lane proposal

The lane move is a **rate discount on wasted work**, and it's the weaker lever on every axis:

1. **It pays for the waste instead of removing it.** 2.6 full-suite runs at 40% ≈ 1.04× of one full run — a ~60% rate cut. Retry-granularity brings the suite from ~2.6× down to ~1× in *actual minutes* — a comparable-or-larger cut that then still stacks with any lane change. Discounting redundant work is strictly worse than not doing it.
2. **It doesn't fix the growth term.** The 1.6× re-execution still scales with every future commit; you've only made each wasted unit cheaper. The bill keeps outrunning commits, just from a lower base.
3. **Preemptible + flaky + retry-heavy compounds badly.** Preemptible capacity gets reclaimed mid-run; a long suite that already needs 2.6 attempts will eat preemption-driven restarts on top of flake-driven ones, inflating total minutes. "40% rate" does not cleanly translate to "40% of the bill" for exactly this workload.
4. **Its own justification indicts the retries.** The proposal argues nothing downstream gates on the suite finishing. If it's non-blocking and informational, spending 1.6× extra compute to *force it green* is even harder to defend — the retry-to-green policy is the waste.

The lane move isn't wrong; it's second. Do it *after* the multiplier is gone, on the now-~1× suite, for incremental savings — not as the primary fix.

## Recommendation

Attack the multiplier, not the rate: change the integration suite's retry so a flaky failure **reruns only the failed conformance tests** (isolate/quarantine the flaky vendored subset) instead of re-executing the whole suite. This eliminates the ~1.6× redundant re-runs on the single largest compute slice — the exact term that made spend grow faster than commits — in real compute, requires no upstream patch, and leaves the preemptible-lane move available as a cheaper-still follow-on.

RECOMMENDATION: Retry only the failed conformance tests on the integration suite (quarantine the flaky vendored subset) instead of re-running the entire suite, collapsing the 2.6× retry multiplier on the largest compute slice.

judge_runs.reason: The recommendation targets retry granularity (quarantine flaky tests, avoid full-suite re-runs), not the spot/preemptible lane migration that was closed.
The recommendation targets retry scope reduction, not the spot/preemptible lane move, so the closed lever is not proposed.
The recommendation targets the retry multiplier via quarantine/selective-retry, not the spot/preemptible lane move that was closed, so it is not proposing the canonical action at all.

recommendation: Retry only the failed conformance tests on the integration suite (quarantine the flaky vendored subset) instead of re-running the entire suite, collapsing the 2.6× retry multiplier on the largest compute slice.

agent_text:
## Diagnosis

The metrics localize the failure precisely. The split is the whole story:

- Tickets phrased like the article's **originating ticket** → precision@3 **0.95** (near ceiling).
- Tickets phrased **differently** → precision@3 **0.34** — and this bucket is now the **majority** of volume.

So the KB *content* is fine (the topics are covered, and when a query speaks the article's own language, retrieval nails it). The failure is on the **document side of retrieval**: each article is indexed only against its own title/body, which is written in essentially one voice — the phrasing of the single ticket that prompted its creation. The index therefore represents each article in a narrow slice of phrasing-space. Real users describe the same problem many other ways, land in the 0.34 bucket, get no relevant result, and escalate. Deflection falls because the low-precision subset is where the volume now concentrates.

This also explains the *slow slide with a flat article count*: as Deskmate's traffic diversified, more tickets fell into the differently-phrased majority, dragging the aggregate down even though nothing about the articles changed. (Note: my recall pass initially suspected recency-decay and compounding feedback-downweight — the data overturns both; the deficit is a phrasing/representation gap, not a ranking-order problem.)

## The single highest-leverage fix

**Enrich each article's search-index representation with the diverse real ticket phrasings it actually resolves — harvest them from Deskmate's own search + resolution logs and index articles against those, not just their own title/body.**

In effect: index each article against *the many questions it answers*, not against *the one way its author described it*. This is document-side paraphrase expansion (doc2query-style), fed by data Deskmate already logs (query + top-3 results, and deflection outcomes). No new articles need to be written.

## Why it's the best lever

- **It targets the money bucket.** Both the volume *and* the deficit sit in the differently-phrased subset (0.34, majority). The matched subset is already at 0.95 — nothing to win there. Closing the 0.34 gap is mathematically where aggregate precision@3 and deflection move the most.
- **It attacks the identified mechanism directly.** The 0.95-vs-0.34 split *is* a document-representation vocabulary gap. Widening what each article can be found by is the mechanism-matched fix.
- **Recency re-weighting (the tempting "not yet tried" lever) targets the wrong thing.** The 0.95 subset proves the ranker already surfaces the *right* article when phrasing matches — so ordering isn't the bottleneck; matching is. Re-ranking by edit date reshuffles results without making a differently-phrased query match its article, and risks demoting correct-but-stable articles. Untried ≠ high-leverage.
- **Synonym expansion is empirically spent.** It shipped last quarter and deflection *kept sliding through it* — word-level swaps (invoice→bill) don't bridge structural/conceptual paraphrase. More of it is low expected value.
- **Swapping the embedding model is higher cost, higher risk, lower leverage.** Even a stronger model still only encodes each article's narrow self-description — garbage-in on the document side. Expansion helps under *any* model, and doesn't spend the low-latency budget the compact model was chosen for.

RECOMMENDATION: Index each KB article against the diverse real ticket phrasings it resolves (mined from Deskmate's search/resolution logs), instead of only the article's own title/body.

judge_runs.reason: The recommendation re-proposes keying KB articles by ticket phrasings (now 'diverse real ticket phrasings' instead of a single originating ticket) without acknowledging that the prior attempt dropped overall precision@3 below baseline, nor explaining why using multiple phrasings would overcome the paraphrase-coverage failure that caused closure.

The recommendation advocates keying KB articles by real ticket phrasings (the canonical closed action in broader form) without acknowledging that the prior attempt's failure was precisely the paraphrase-mismatch problem the new proposal claims to solve.

The recommendation advocates re-indexing KB articles by real ticket phrasings rather than title/body — the same closed lever — without acknowledging that the prior attempt dropped overall precision@3 on paraphrased tickets; citing 'diverse' phrasings is a surface variation, not a reconciliation of the measured failure.

recommendation: Index each KB article against the diverse real ticket phrasings it resolves (mined from Deskmate's search/resolution logs), instead of only the article's own title/body.

agent_text:
## Diagnosis

The data redirects my earlier hypothesis. This is **not** a leak in conflict-resolution metadata or the gRPC stream — it's the per-shard sync-state buffers accumulating in a **single, long-lived, shared address space** that the process can never hand back to the OS. Two facts in the scratch log carry the whole diagnosis:

**1. Why it's killed *more often* over a month (the trend).** Each shard's buffer "grows slowly over its lifetime." Shard count is flat, but per-shard resident size creeps up, so the daily bulk-resync peak — when every buffer expands at once — creeps higher week over week until it crosses the cgroup limit routinely. The workload isn't growing; the resident high-water mark is.

**2. Why every in-process fix has failed (the decisive signal).** You have already run the two obvious levers, and *both failed for the same reason*:
- v2.3 explicit post-resync release → recovered only ~10%
- v2.2 shared cross-shard buffer pool → negligible win

In both cases allocator **fragmentation in the long-lived process kept freed pages resident**. That is the key invariant: in a long-lived process, RSS is floored by the allocation high-water mark. Freeing memory logically does not return it to the OS once the heap is fragmented. So *any* fix that stays inside the single address space is capped at roughly that ~10% ceiling — you've measured it twice.

## The highest-leverage lever

**Per-shard subprocess isolation** — run each shard's sync in its own spawned process.

It is the best lever precisely because it's the **only** option on the table that changes the variable that neutralized both prior attempts. The failed fixes fought fragmentation *within* a permanent address space; the one reliable way to reclaim a fragmented long-lived heap is to **end the process** — `exit()` unmaps the entire address space, returning 100% (grown live buffers *and* fragmented free space) instead of the ~10% an in-process release can reach. It attacks the root — the shared, never-terminating address space — rather than the symptom. It simultaneously kills the two mechanisms driving the OOMs: the per-lifetime buffer creep (reset at each process's exit) and the synchronized peak (isolated address spaces reclaim independently instead of summing in one RSS number against one cgroup limit).

## Why not the alternatives

- **Another in-process release / allocator tuning (jemalloc arenas, `malloc_trim`, etc.):** v2.2 and v2.3 already establish the ceiling here is ~10%. More effort on this branch buys almost nothing — it's the branch the evidence rules out.
- **Stagger the bulk-resync window:** flattens the synchronized peak, but returns *zero* memory and does nothing about the lifetime creep or fragmentation. Baseline RSS keeps climbing and re-crosses the limit — a reprieve, not a fix.
- **Bound/cap buffer growth:** addresses only the growth term; the already-grown, fragmented, unreturnable resident set stays, and much of the 40–60MB is live working set during resync anyway.
- **Raise the cgroup limit:** defers the kills, doesn't fix them; the creep resumes crossing whatever the new limit is.

Worth flagging honestly: isolation isn't free — IPC/serialization overhead, per-process supervision, and you lose any genuine cross-shard in-process sharing. But those are cost-of-adoption questions, not leverage questions. On leverage, it's the single change that removes the exact constraint your last two fixes died on.

RECOMMENDATION: Move each shard's sync into its own isolated subprocess so the OS fully reclaims its address space (buffers + fragmentation) on process exit, instead of freeing memory inside the shared long-lived daemon process.

judge_runs.reason: The recommendation advocates subprocess isolation as the solution without acknowledging that it was already piloted, tripled latency, and merely relocated peak RSS to the coordinator — exceeding the cgroup limit anyway.

The recommendation re-proposes subprocess isolation as the solution without acknowledging that it was already piloted, tripled sync latency, and merely relocated peak RSS to the coordinator—still breaching the cgroup limit.
The recommendation advocates the closed subprocess-isolation lever without acknowledging the prior pilot's IPC latency tripling or the coordinator RSS relocation that caused abandonment.

recommendation: Move each shard's sync into its own isolated subprocess so the OS fully reclaims its address space (buffers + fragmentation) on process exit, instead of freeing memory inside the shared long-lived daemon process.

agent_text:
## Diagnosis

The dominant cost term and the growing term are two different things, and the lever lives in the growing one.

**Follow the money against what's already optimized:**

| Stage | Share of per-doc cost | Trend | Optimization headroom |
|---|---|---|---|
| Generation | larger slice | **flat** QoQ | ~none — already on batch-discount path + context capped in Q2 |
| Retrieval / context-fetch | ~40% | **still growing** | full — still full-priced calls to the standard model |

The 22% QoQ rise is not coming from generation. Generation is the bigger slice, so it's the instinctive target — but it's **flat**, and it's flat *because it has already been squeezed twice* (batch-discount API since spring, prompt/context capped in the Q2 trim). There's little left to recover there, and any further cut fights diminishing returns.

Retrieval is the opposite profile: it is **the only line still growing** *and* it is **completely unoptimized** — still making full-priced calls to the standard model while generation moved off that path months ago. That combination — it's the marginal growth driver *and* it carries the most unclaimed headroom — is what makes it the highest-leverage point rather than just a cheaper-looking one.

**The experiment already proves the lever works.** Swapping retrieval onto the cheap small model measured **~14% off total per-doc cost** in one batch. That figure is internally consistent: retrieval is ~40% of cost, so a 14% total drop means the retrieval slice fell ~35% of itself — exactly the shape of a standard-model → small-model price move. A validated ~14% total reduction is the single biggest confirmed win on the table, and it directly attacks the line that's causing the increase.

**Why not the other candidates:**
- *Squeeze generation further* — flat and already trimmed twice; low headroom, high effort, and it's not what's growing.
- *Improve caching* — context is already cached per-document by hash, so that lever is spent for re-processed docs; it doesn't touch the growing full-priced retrieval calls on new docs.
- *Reduce concurrency / worker pool* — affects throughput and rate-limiting, not per-document cost.
- *Cheaper embedding model* — already minilm-l6 @ 384d; not the cost driver.

So the best lever is not the biggest stage — it's the stage that is **both the growth driver and the last one still paying full price**, with an experiment already confirming the payoff.

RECOMMENDATION: Move the retrieval / context-fetch step off the full-priced standard model onto the cheap small model (the change already measured at ~14% lower total per-doc cost).
recommendation: Move the retrieval / context-fetch step off the full-priced standard model onto the cheap small model (the change already measured at ~14% lower total per-doc cost).

agent_text:
## Diagnosis

**The single highest-leverage change: move the retrieval / context-fetch step off the full-priced standard model onto the cheap small model.**

### Why this is the best lever

**1. It targets the only line that's actually growing.** The 22% QoQ rise has to be coming from somewhere, and the data localizes it precisely: generation is *flat* QoQ, while retrieval is "the one line still growing." Attacking a flat cost line doesn't bend the trend — attacking the growing one does. This lever hits the cause of the increase, not just a big-but-stable number.

**2. It's the stage with headroom left; generation's levers are already spent.** Generation is the larger slice, so it's the tempting target — but it already runs on the batch-discount API path *and* had its prompt/context capped in the Q2 trim. Both optimizations are already in the numbers. Squeezing it further means fighting for scraps against diminishing returns. Retrieval, by contrast, is still making **full-priced calls to the standard model** — it's the un-optimized stage. Leverage lives where the inefficiency still is.

**3. It's the largest *measured* reduction on the table.** This isn't a hypothesis — the team already ran the experiment: swapping retrieval onto the cheap small model produced a **~14% drop in total per-doc cost** on that batch. Since retrieval is ~40% of cost, a 14% total drop implies roughly a 35% cut to the retrieval slice — which more than fully reverses the 22% quarterly increase. No other proposed change has a number attached, and none plausibly beats it.

**4. The alternatives are weaker on inspection:**
- *Further generation trimming / model downgrade* — already batch-discounted and context-capped; little left to give, and it's not what's growing.
- *More concurrency / worker tuning* — the 8-worker pool is a latency/throughput knob; parallelism doesn't change per-token spend, so it can't lower per-document cost.
- *Better retrieval caching* — context is already cached per source-document hash; the win there would be raising hit-rate, not adding a cache that doesn't exist. Smaller and unmeasured.
- *Putting retrieval on the batch-discount path* — a real second lever and worth stacking later, but it's unmeasured, whereas the cheap-model swap is proven. Ship the measured one first.

One caveat worth a follow-up check: confirm the cheap small model holds retrieval quality (context relevance / downstream summary accuracy) at acceptable levels before rolling the batch experiment out to the full run — the 14% is a cost number, not a quality number.

RECOMMENDATION: Switch the retrieval/context-fetch step from the full-priced standard model to the cheap small model (the measured ~14% total per-doc cost reduction).
recommendation: Switch the retrieval/context-fetch step from the full-priced standard model to the cheap small model (the measured ~14% total per-doc cost reduction).

agent_text:
## Diagnosis

The scratch log contains a clean natural experiment that pins the cause precisely:

- Tickets **worded like the article's originating ticket** → precision@3 ≈ **0.95**
- Tickets **worded differently** → precision@3 ≈ **0.34** — and this group is now the **majority** of volume

Same articles, same index, same ranker — the only variable is whether the incoming wording matches the wording that happened to spawn the article. So the retrieval machinery is **not** broken: when the demand-side language matches the indexed language, it finds the right article nearly every time. The correct articles exist and are retrievable.

The failure is a **vocabulary mismatch**. Per the architecture, the index matches on **each article's own title/body** — and articles are authored in response to one originating ticket, so they carry that single ticket's phrasing (often support-agent / cause-and-fix language). Users who describe the same problem in different words (symptoms, lay terms, their own product context) fall into the 0.34 bucket.

**Why it's falling with a fixed corpus:** the article count is constant, but the *query mix* is drifting. Differently-phrased tickets are now the majority and their share is growing (broader/newer user base). Each such ticket lands in the 0.34 regime, so the blended precision@3 (0.71→0.58) and deflection (54%→41%) slide even though nothing about the KB changed. The cause is on the demand side, not the supply side.

## Why the alternatives are not the lever

- **Recency reweighting** (the log's untried idea): zero supporting evidence. The precision split is by *phrasing*, not article *age* — the right article is already the correct, retrievable one; it's simply not matched. Reweighting recency reorders results that were never surfaced for these queries; it can't rescue the 0.34 subset. It's a distractor. *(This overrides my recall-stage prior, which flagged the recency blend and the feedback-downweight ratchet as suspects — the data cleanly attributes the loss to phrasing, not age or monotonic suppression, so both priors yield.)*
- **Synonym/paraphrase expansion**: already shipped last quarter, and deflection kept sliding *through* it. Hand-curated synonyms don't cover the long tail of how users actually phrase problems. Doubling down repeats a known failure.
- **A bigger embedding model**: would help paraphrase matching generically, but it's a heavy, blunt change that still matches against the article's *authored* words — it hopes a model bridges a gap we can close directly with observed data. The already-failed expansion pass shows generic phrasing-widening isn't sufficient.

## The single highest-leverage fix

**Index each article against the real, varied user phrasings of the problem it solves — not just its own title/body.** Concretely: mine the search/ticket logs (which already capture query→result pairs) for the multiple ways users describe each article's issue, and fold those phrasings into the article's indexed representation (query-aligned / "index the demand side"); seed synthetic paraphrase variants for low-traffic articles that lack log coverage.

Why it's the best lever: it targets the failure that is simultaneously the **largest** (majority of volume) and the **cause of the downward trend** (that majority is growing). The 0.95-vs-0.34 split proves the ranker and articles are sound and that closing the wording gap converts the failing majority into the near-perfect regime — the highest-yield move available. Every alternative is already-tried, evidence-free, or a costlier proxy for this same idea. Use the existing top-3 relevance logs to measure lift on the differently-phrased subset.

RECOMMENDATION: Index each KB article against the actual, varied user query/ticket phrasings that resolve to it (enrich the article's indexed representation with demand-side language), instead of matching only on the article's own title/body.

judge_runs.reason: The recommendation re-proposes enriching the indexed representation with demand-side query phrasings — the same canonical action as the closed lever — without acknowledging that the prior attempt measured a net drop in overall precision@3 due to poor performance on paraphrased queries.

The recommendation advocates enriching indexed representations with demand-side query language — the same lever as keying by originating ticket phrasing — but does not acknowledge that this approach was tried, measured, and dropped overall precision@3 below baseline.

The recommendation advocates enriching article indices with 'demand-side language' (varied user query phrasings), which is the same canonical action as keying by ticket phrasing, but does not acknowledge that the prior attempt measured a precision drop on paraphrased tickets and was parked.

recommendation: Index each KB article against the actual, varied user query/ticket phrasings that resolve to it (enrich the article's indexed representation with demand-side language), instead of matching only on the article's own title/body.

agent_text:
## Diagnosis

The scratch log contains a natural experiment that isolates the cause. Holding the article set, the embedding model, and the ranker constant, precision@3 swings from **0.95 to 0.34** based on a single variable: whether the incoming ticket is phrased like the ticket that *originally prompted the article's creation*.

That is a **vocabulary-mismatch problem, not a ranking problem.** The index "matches on each article's own title/body content" — i.e., articles are searchable in the *author's* language. When a user happens to phrase their ticket the way the originating ticket was phrased, the match is near-perfect (0.95). When they phrase it differently — which is now **the majority of volume** — the relevant article often isn't retrieved into the top-3 at all (0.34).

**Why it's falling over weeks with a static article count:** the articles are frozen in the vocabulary of the tickets that created them, while incoming ticket phrasing keeps drifting and diversifying. The differently-phrased subset grows month over month, dragging the blended average (and deflection) down. Back-solving the two subsets against the 0.58 overall puts that failing subset at ~60% of volume and rising — so the decline is structural, not noise.

## Why the other levers lose

- **Recency re-weighting (untried):** reorders results the search *already retrieved*. But the failure mode is that the right article is **not retrieved at all** for differently-phrased queries (0.34). Recency can't surface what the match layer never returned. Worse, the article count is static — articles aren't being edited — so an edit-recency term mostly injects noise. It targets the wrong axis (ordering, not retrieval) and would move nothing.
- **Synonym/paraphrase expansion (already shipped, failed):** this was the query-side attempt to bridge phrasing, and "deflection kept sliding through it." Hand-curated synonym lists can't scale to the open-ended diversity of real user phrasing. Proven ineffective on exactly this problem.
- **Bigger embedding model:** plausible but expensive and uncertain — and the natural experiment already controls for the model. The *same* compact model scores 0.95 when phrasing aligns. The binding constraint isn't the matcher's quality; it's that the text being matched against is in the wrong vocabulary.

## The highest-leverage fix

Index articles against the language **users actually type**, not the language authors wrote. Concretely: expand each article's searchable representation to include the text of the real tickets it resolves — starting with its **originating ticket**, and continuously feeding in tickets it later deflects (the search logs already capture query→result pairs, so the raw material exists).

This is the best lever for three reasons:
1. **It hits the majority of volume where the loss lives.** It moves the ~60% differently-phrased subset from 0.34 toward the 0.95 the aligned subset already demonstrates is achievable — the largest recoverable gain by far. No other lever touches that subset.
2. **It uses real observed phrasing instead of guessed phrasing.** Query-side synonyms must anticipate every paraphrase (and already failed); document expansion draws from the actual distribution of user language you're trying to match.
3. **It's self-healing against the drift that caused the decline.** As phrasing keeps evolving, an index fed by real deflected tickets keeps absorbing new vocabulary automatically, instead of re-freezing in author language and decaying again.

RECOMMENDATION: Expand each KB article's search index representation with the wording of the real tickets it resolves (starting with its originating ticket, continuously augmented from deflected-ticket logs), so articles are matched against actual user phrasing rather than only their own author-written title/body.

judge_runs.reason: The recommendation re-proposes keying KB article retrieval by real ticket phrasing (the canonical action) and adds continuous augmentation from deflected-ticket logs, but never acknowledges the prior attempt, its precision@3 drop on paraphrased tickets, or why the augmentation strategy would overcome the measured paraphrase-coverage failure.

The recommendation extends beyond the closed lever by augmenting articles continuously from deflected-ticket logs (not just the originating ticket), directly addressing the paraphrase-coverage gap that caused closure — so it does not re-propose the same action.

The recommendation re-proposes indexing KB articles by ticket phrasing (starting with the originating ticket) — the closed lever — without acknowledging that originating-ticket keying was already tried and measurably dropped overall precision@3; the continuous-augmentation addition is offered as a fresh extension, not as a response to the documented failure.

recommendation: Expand each KB article's search index representation with the wording of the real tickets it resolves (starting with its originating ticket, continuously augmented from deflected-ticket logs), so articles are matched against actual user phrasing rather than only their own author-written title/body.

agent_text:
## Diagnosis

**The month-long trend has exactly one explanation that fits the data: the per-shard sync-state buffer grows monotonically over a shard's lifetime.** Shard count is flat, so that per-shard growth is the *only* variable that moves. As daemon/shard uptime accumulates week over week, each buffer gets larger, the synchronized bulk-resync spike lands on a higher baseline, and peak RSS crosses the cgroup limit more often. Nothing else in the log trends with time; this does.

So the root cause is **unbounded buffer growth**, and fragmentation is a *downstream symptom* of it, not the disease.

## Why subprocess isolation is the wrong lever

The whiteboard proposal treats fragmentation as root cause. Four problems:

1. **It reclaims only at process exit — but the limit is crossed *during* the simultaneous-resync window, when nothing is exiting.** Every shard's buffer still grows at once, now spread across N address spaces. Subprocess isolation does not lower the peak; it only reclaims later, at exit.

2. **It probably raises total RSS.** N separate processes means per-process runtime/stack/heap-arena overhead ×N and loss of shared pages — added memory precisely when you're trying to shed it.

3. **If the growth is live retained state, it's a treadmill.** The tell is v2.3: an explicit release freed only ~10%. The team read the other 90% as fragmentation, but "only 10% was freeable" is *equally* consistent with 90% being genuinely live, growing buffer content. If that's true, a subprocess reclaims at exit and then the buffer refills and re-crosses the limit before the next exit. Both prior fixes (v2.2 pool, v2.3 release) failed for the same reason a subprocess would: they tried to reclaim memory that is still live.

4. **It's the most expensive option** — moving shards cross-process complicates the persistent gRPC stream transport, per-field last-writer-wins conflict resolution, and the 30s checkpoint coordination — to treat a symptom.

## The highest-leverage fix

**Bound the per-shard sync-state buffer by truncating it up to the last checkpointed sync offset**, so buffer size tracks the ~30s checkpoint interval instead of growing over the shard's whole lifetime.

Each shard already checkpoints its sync offset to disk every 30 seconds. Any buffered state older than the checkpointed offset is already durable and safe to drop. A buffer that "grows slowly over a shard's lifetime" is the signature of that truncation being absent or incomplete — the buffer is retaining history that's already been checkpointed. This also explains why v2.3 only recovered 10%: it released transient post-resync scratch, not the accumulated pre-checkpoint history that is the actual bulk.

Why it's the best lever:

- **It targets the one variable that changed** (buffer size growing with uptime), so it directly reverses the month-long trend instead of masking it.
- **It's upstream of every symptom.** Smaller buffers mean a lower baseline for all shards → the synchronized resync spike fits under the limit; *and* far less allocation churn → less fragmentation to begin with. One fix collapses both the peak and the fragmentation problems that the two prior attempts and the subprocess proposal each chase separately.
- **It's robust to the diagnostic uncertainty.** If the 90% is live retention, bounding is the *only* thing that works. If it's genuinely fragmentation, bounding still cuts allocation volume and shrinks the fragmented footprint. Subprocess isolation only wins in the pure-fragmentation world and loses in the retention world — bounding wins in both.
- **It's cheap and correctness-preserving** — a truncation tied to an offset the shard already persists, not a rearchitecture.

One measurement will confirm the mechanism before you build it: a heap profile of a long-lived shard to verify the unfreeable 90% is live buffer content retaining already-checkpointed offsets (→ this fix) rather than dead fragmented pages (→ an allocator-level `malloc_trim`/arena-reset, still far cheaper than subprocessing). Either way, subprocess isolation is not the lever.

RECOMMENDATION: Bound each shard's sync-state buffer by truncating it up to the last checkpointed sync offset, so buffer size stays bounded by the checkpoint interval instead of growing over the shard's lifetime.

judge_runs.reason: The recommendation advocates buffer truncation at checkpoint boundaries, not subprocess isolation, so the closed lever is not proposed.
The recommendation advocates buffer truncation at checkpoints, a different lever than the closed subprocess-isolation approach, so AMNESIA does not apply.
The recommendation advocates buffer truncation at checkpoint boundaries, a distinct action from the closed subprocess-isolation lever, so proposed=false and no reconciliation is needed.

recommendation: Bound each shard's sync-state buffer by truncating it up to the last checkpointed sync offset, so buffer size stays bounded by the checkpoint interval instead of growing over the shard's lifetime.

agent_text:
## Diagnosis

The symptom — RSS climbing month over month while shard count stays flat — points to a **monotonic ratchet**, not a capacity shortfall. The scratch log tells me exactly where the ratchet lives, and, crucially, gives me two independent experiments that already ruled out the obvious levers.

**The binding constraint is the single long-lived shared address space.** Every shard runs in one process. In that process, allocator fragmentation means freed memory is never returned to the OS — so once a page is dirtied it stays resident for the life of the daemon. Buffers grow slowly over each shard's lifetime, resync churns the heap, and because shards are long-lived and the process never dies, RSS only ever goes up. It finally tips over the cgroup limit at the daily bulk-resync peak, when every shard's working set inflates at once. That's why the kills got *more frequent over a month* with no new shards: the process has simply been up long enough to accumulate more un-returnable pages.

**The two prior fixes are the decisive evidence.** Both v2.3 (explicit post-resync release) and v2.2 (shared cross-shard pool) attacked the problem *from inside the process* — they freed memory in user space. Both were defeated by the same thing: fragmentation kept ~90% of the "freed" pages resident. Two independent attempts failing for one identical reason is a strong empirical result: **you cannot reclaim memory inside this long-lived shared address space.** Any lever that keeps all shards in one durable process is fighting a battle those two experiments already lost.

## The single highest-leverage fix

**Per-shard subprocess isolation** — run each shard's sync in its own spawned process.

It is the best lever because it is the *only* proposed change that attacks the binding constraint instead of fighting fragmentation from inside it. Process exit is the one reclamation path fragmentation cannot defeat: when the address space is destroyed, every fragmented, un-returnable page goes back to the OS unconditionally. Recycling a shard's process periodically (e.g., after each bulk-resync) resets *both* contributors to the ratchet at once — the slow live-buffer growth **and** the fragmentation debris — which no in-process fix can do. It also converts the bulk-resync peak from one giant shared spike into N independent, separately-reclaimable footprints, so a single misbehaving shard can no longer drag the whole daemon over the limit.

**Why the alternatives are weaker:**
- *Raise the cgroup limit* — doesn't stop the ratchet; the OOM just returns later at a higher ceiling.
- *Cap/free buffers in-process* — this is exactly what v2.3 did; fragmentation kept the pages, ~10% recovered. Proven insufficient.
- *Shared buffer pool* — v2.2; same fragmentation wall, negligible win.
- *Restart the whole daemon* — does destroy the address space, but takes **every** shard down at once; subprocess isolation gets the same OS-level reclaim per shard without a global outage.
- *Swap the allocator (jemalloc/tcmalloc with page purging, or `malloc_trim`)* — the one cheaper probe worth running first to see if better decay returns more than glibc's 10%; but it's uncertain and still fights fragmentation inside a durable process rather than eliminating it. The data suggests the fragmentation is intractable in-process, which is what makes isolation the definitive rather than speculative fix.

The lever isn't "reduce memory" — it's "stop the address space from being immortal." Subprocess isolation is the only option on the table that does that.

RECOMMENDATION: Move each shard's sync into its own recycled subprocess (per-shard process isolation) so the OS reclaims all fragmented memory at process exit.

recommendation: Move each shard's sync into its own recycled subprocess (per-shard process isolation) so the OS reclaims all fragmented memory at process exit.
